entry.title: Cutover control for SAP Cloud Integration: CSV driven bulk shutdown and wave based start of iFlows
entry.content:
INTRODUCTION

I am working on a large ECC to S/4HANA migration where the SAP Cloud Integration tenant stays the same .Existing iFlows are repointed to S/4HANA rather than rebuilt. The cutover therefore requires interfaces talking to ECC to be stopped before data migration, repointed, and brought back up in a controlled sequence. Crucially, only the iFlows touching the migrating SAP system need to stop; the same tenant runs third party and non-SAP interfaces that must keep running. So "undeploy everything" is not an option . What is needed is selective undeploy and deploy of a defined list and this is exactly what this tool does.

On SAP PI/PO this was a solved problem: PIMON let you filter, multi-select and stop/start channels centrally, with status visible in the same place. CPI has no equivalent . The web UI handles one artifact at a time, which in a cutover window is slow and error prone. The model also differs: PI stopped the channel while the scenario stayed in place; in CPI the lever is undeploying the artifact from the runtime — design untouched, but stopping means removing the deployed artifact.

REQUIREMENT


The case for a tool comes down to two numbers — time and error rate. At real cutover scale, say 250 iFlows in scope:

  1. Reduce time. Manually, each undeploy is filter, select, click Undeploy, confirm, verify — 30 to 45 seconds per iFlow on a responsive tenant. That is close to 3 hours of pure clicking for 250 iFlows, and with scope cross-checking, evidence capture and interruptions, realistically 4 to 6 hours, usually split across several people — which adds its own coordination risk of items falling between the splits. The tool executes the same 250 undeploys via the API in under 10 minutes.

  2. Reduce error rate. Human accuracy on a repetitive 250-item selection task over hours is not 100%. Even a 1–2% slip rate means 2 to 5 wrong outcomes: an in-scope iFlow missed and still pumping messages into ECC during data migration, or an out-of-scope one undeployed — taking down a live interface that had nothing to do with the cutover. Worst of all, manual misses are silent. With the tool, the scope is a reviewed CSV and the follow-up -status run is a complete, deterministic audit of all 250 — not a memory of what got clicked.

    This blog describes a command line tool I built to solve this .The setup is:

    CSV list of iFlows --> Java CLI tool --> CPI OData API (v1) --> CPI runtime

    During cutover we needed:

    1. A reviewed and signed off list of in-scope iFlows

    2. A status check before the shutdown window (everything STARTED)

    3. A bulk undeploy of exactly that list

    4. A status check after (everything gone from runtime

    5. Console output that can be pasted into the cutover log as evidence

Only the interfaces in scope should be touched. Other integrations on the tenant keep running.


THE TOOL

The tool is a single jar Java 21 application taking three arguments:

java -jar cpi-iflow-controller-1.1.0.jar <config-file> <iflows-csv> <-status|-undeploy|-deploy>

The iFlow list is a two column CSV — package and iFlow name:

Package,iFlow Name
PACKAGEMONTHLYEXTRACTINTERFACES,MONTHLY_TAX_DATA
PACKAGEOTCOBJECTS,OTC_BILLING_DATA
PACKAGEOTCOBJECTS,OTC_MASTER_DATA
PACKAGEOTCOBJECTS,OTC_FI_DATA


Note the Package column contains technical package Ids.

When the cutover task says "stop in-scope interfaces", the definition of in-scope is this file.

 

GOTCHA : Package name and Package Id are not the same thing


This one cost me a debugging session. I first populated the CSV from the Manage Integration Content monitor, where every deployed iFlow shows a Package field:

The first run failed for every iFlow with "iFlow not found in package" — even though the monitor showed all of them as Started:




The reason: the Package field in the monitor is the package display name, but the OData API keys on the technical package Id — and the two differ. The Id is auto-generated from the name with special characters (including underscores) stripped, so a package named PACKAGE_OTC_OBJECTS has the Id PACKAGEOTCOBJECTS:



The same applies to artifacts — an iFlow has a display name and a technical Id, and the API wants the Id. In my case the two matched (the monitor shows both, under Name and ID), but do not assume they do.

Where to find the technical Ids:

  • Open the package in Design — the Package ID is on the Overview tab and in the browser URL (.../shell/design/contentpackage/<Id>)

  • Or query the API directly:

curl -s -H "Authorization: Bearer $TOKEN" \

"https://<tmn-host>/api/v1/IntegrationPackages?$select=Id,Name&$format=json"

With the technical Ids in the CSV, the same run succeeds:

One more useful behaviour: for -status the package column can be left empty. The runtime API only needs the iFlow Id, so the tool skips package resolution and queries IntegrationRuntimeArtifacts directly:

Rule of thumb: the CSV takes technical Ids, not the display names in the monitor. For status-only runs the package column is optional; for deploy it must be correct, because the design-time lookup depends on it.

CHECKING STATUS

java -jar target/cpi-iflow-controller-1.1.0.jar config.properties iflows.csv -status

This prints the runtime status of each iFlow in the CSV:

+---------+---------------------------------+------------------+
| STATUS | PACKAGE | IFLOW |
+---------+---------------------------------+------------------+
| STARTED | PACKAGEMONTHLYEXTRACTINTERFACES | MONTHLY_TAX_DATA |
| STARTED | PACKAGEOTCOBJECTS | OTC_BILLING_DATA |
| STARTED | PACKAGEOTCOBJECTS | OTC_MASTER_DATA |
| STARTED | PACKAGEOTCOBJECTS | OTC_FI_DATA |
+---------+---------------------------------+------------------+
Summary: 4 succeeded, 0 failed (4 total)

Status is run at two points in the cutover: before the shutdown window to confirm the baseline (everything STARTED), and after the undeploy to confirm the interfaces are down. The two captures become the before/after evidence for the runbook verification task. Beyond cutover, the same command doubles as a quick health check across a defined interface list — after a tenant update or during hypercare. A useful side effect: it surfaces artifacts already in ERROR state, which you want to find before the cutover window, not during it.

UNDEPLOYING

java -jar target/cpi-iflow-controller-1.1.0.jar config.properties iflows.csv -undeploy

This walks the same CSV and undeploys each artifact via the OData v1 API (IntegrationRuntimeArtifacts). The summary line shows immediately if anything failed.
An important detail: undeploy is asynchronous in CPI. The OData call only submits the request — the API accepts it and returns, while removal from the runtime node happens in the background. A successful call means "the stop was requested", not "the interface is down". If an iFlow is mid-message, the runtime winds that down first. This is visible in Manage Integration Content right after the bulk undeploy — all four iFlows sit in Stopping state.

This is exactly why status matters in the shutdown sequence. After -undeploy, run -status against the same CSV; if some artifacts still show up, wait and re-run until the list is clear. The evidence that satisfies the verification task is the status output showing the interfaces are down — not the undeploy summary showing requests were submitted. The undeploy is the trigger; the status check is the confirmation.

Configuration:

Connectivity uses a properties file for the credentials

cpi.base.url=https://your-tenant.it-cpi018.cfapps.eu10.hana.ondemand.com
cpi.auth.type=oauth2
cpi.oauth.token.url=https://your-tenant.authentication.eu10.hana.ondemand.com/oauth/token
cpi.oauth.client.id=your-client-id
cpi.oauth.client.secret=your-client-secret


One config file per tenant: the same CSV is exercised against test during cutover rehearsal, then replayed against production by changing a single argument.

BTP service instance, plan and roles

The service key comes from a Process Integration Runtime instance with the api plan (not *integration-flow*, which is only for calling deployed iFlows). Create the instance in the BTP cockpit under Services > Service Marketplace, select the roles at creation, and create a service key with grant type client_credentials. The clientid, clientsecret, tokenurl and url map directly to config.properties above.

Three roles are needed, mapped to what each mode does:

Role

Used for

MonitoringDataRead

-status — reading runtime artifact status via IntegrationRuntimeArtifacts

WorkspacePackagesRead

Resolving iFlows inside a package at design time (IntegrationPackages / IntegrationDesigntimeArtifacts)

WorkspaceArtifactsDeploy

-deploy and -undeploy — deploying and undeploying artifacts

These are set as parameters at instance creation. The exact configuration the tool has been verified against:

The token-validity of 43200 seconds gives a 12 hour token — longer than any cutover window, so the tool requests one token per run and never refreshes mid-sequence. Role names per API operation are listed in the SAP Help Portal under "Tasks and Permissions" for the Cloud Integration OData API.

A recommendation from experience: create a dedicated instance and key with only these roles, rather than reusing a broad integration developer key or an AuthGroup role — least privilege applies to cutover tooling too. For status-only runs (e.g. a hypercare health check), MonitoringDataRead and WorkspacePackagesRead are enough. And keep the filled-in config.properties out of Git — the repo commits templates only.

Controlled start: using deploy mode in waves

Deployment

The tool also supports a deploy mode:

java -jar target/cpi-iflow-controller-1.1.0.jar config.properties iflows-wave1.csv -deploy

However, shutdown and startup are not used the same way. In this migration the deploy step matters even more: between shutdown and startup the iFlows are repointed from ECC to S/4HANA — connection details, externalized parameters and endpoints updated — so each wave brings up the reconfigured interface, validated wave by wave against the new environment.

Shutdown is a blanket action — everything in scope must be down before migration starts, so one bulk undeploy of the full CSV is exactly right. Startup is a controlled start — interfaces return in a deliberate sequence, typically master data flows first and then transactional, gated by smoke tests and sign-offs between waves. Starting everything in one shot would flood a freshly migrated system with queued messages before anyone has validated the first interface.
At the same time, a wave is rarely a single interface — a master data wave might hold a dozen iFlows that come up together, and deploying those one by one through the UI has the same problem as the shutdown, just smaller. So the same CSV driven approach applies, with one CSV per wave:

Running: deploy with a wave CSV brings up all its iFlows in one command. Deploy is asynchronous like undeploy — the call submits, the runtime takes time to start — so the pattern repeats: -deploy the wave, then -status against the same CSV until everything shows STARTED, then run the smoke tests. Only after sign-off does the next wave go up.

The exercise on a trial 

I ran the full sequence on my tenant with the four iFlows split into two waves: all four stopped in one bulk undeploy (the Stopping screenshot above), then wave 1 (the three OTC iFlows) and wave 2 (MONTHLY_TAX_DATA) started separately.

Deploying wave 1 brings up all three OTC iFlows in one command — the DEPLOYING result confirms the requests were accepted

The asynchronous start is visible in the UI moments later — OTC_BILLING_DATA is already Started while the other two are still Starting:

Once the runtime finishes, -status against wave1.csv confirms all three STARTED — this is the check before wave 1 smoke tests:

 

Only then does wave 2 go up the same way:

Sequencing between waves stays a runbook decision. The tool automates everything within a wave — one command instead of a dozen clicks, with the status check confirming it.

GitHub Dev Containers

For this tool, and for development in general

The part I want to highlight is the environment. The classic failure mode of a utility like this: it works on the author's laptop, but the person executing the cutover task has JDK 17 instead of 21, or no Maven, or a proxy issue — and a five minute task becomes an hour of environment debugging inside the cutover window.

The repo ships with a .devcontainer definition: a small JSON file (plus optionally a Dockerfile) committed to the repo, describing the complete development environment — runtime versions, build tools, CLI utilities, VS Code extensions and settings. Open the repo and VS Code builds a container from it; your editor works inside that container.

How this differs from a traditional IDE setup

With a traditional IDE setup (Eclipse, IntelliJ, or plain VS Code on the host), the environment lives on the machine and only the project lives in the repo. JDK version, Maven settings, proxy config and plugins are per-machine state set up by hand, usually from a stale wiki page. Two people on the same project routinely have different environments, and nobody can prove which is correct.

With a dev container, the environment lives in the repo, next to the code:

  • The environment is versioned. When the tool moves to a newer Java version, the dev container moves in the same commit. Checking out an old tag gives you the old environment too — no traditional IDE setup offers that.

  • Onboarding is one click. Clone, "Reopen in Container", done. No install guide, no "which JDK" thread. Extensions and settings come along, so even the editor is consistent.

  • The host stays clean. Projects with conflicting toolchains (Java 17 here, Java 21 there, different Node versions for CAP projects) coexist without nvm/sdkman juggling — each project brings its own container.

  • It runs in the browser. GitHub Codespaces runs the same definition on a cloud VM with zero local install — genuinely useful when the person on cutover duty has a locked down laptop where installing a JDK needs a service ticket.

The trade-offs are small: Docker Desktop must be installed and licensed on the host (Codespaces avoids even this), the first build takes a few minutes, and Windows file I/O is slower unless the repo lives in WSL2. For most SAP side-car development these costs are negligible against the consistency gained.

This isn't unique to this tool. For a great walkthrough of the same pattern applied to SAP CAP development - showing how one dev container definition runs identically across local VS Code, GitHub Codespaces, and Business Application Studio - see DJ Adams' Developing CAP in containers – three ways.

Dev containers as a development practice, not just for this tool

I now treat the dev container as part of the deliverable for anything I build outside the ABAP stack, and it fits BTP development well — CAP (Node.js and Java), the supporting tooling that accumulates around a CPI workstream, and UI5/Fiori all benefit from pinned runtime and tooling versions, removing the "works for me" drift between developers entirely.

ABAP itself stays in the ABAP stack — but nearly everything around it in a modern SAP landscape is Java or Node, and all of it can be containerised this way.

For SAP integration teams this matters more than in mainstream web development. The team mixes ABAP developers, middleware specialists and functional consultants — you cannot assume a curated local Java setup. The dev container turns "build and run this Java tool" into a task anyone can execute the same way.

The cutover rehearsal workflow

Open the repo in Codespaces (or reopen locally in the dev container)

mvn clean package --> target/cpi-iflow-controller-1.1.0.jar
Copy the config and CSV templates; fill in tenant details and the signed off interface list

Run -status, capture, run -undeploy, run -status again, capture

Paste both tables into the cutover log

Conclusion

  • Cutover integration shutdown becomes deterministic with a CSV driven bulk undeploy instead of clicking through the CPI UI.
  • Package name and package Id are different — the monitor shows display names, the OData API wants technical Ids (auto-generated with special characters stripped). Populate the CSV with Ids.
  • The CSV doubles as the signed off shutdown scope; the before/after status tables double as cutover evidence.
    Undeploy is asynchronous in CPI — the call submits the stop request; the status check confirms the interfaces are down. Undeploy is the trigger, status is the evidence.
  • Ramp-up uses the same tool in deploy mode with wave specific CSVs — all iFlows in a wave deploy together in one command, while sequencing between waves (master data first, then transactional) stays a runbook decision gated by smoke tests and sign-offs.
  • A GitHub Dev Container means anyone can build and run the tool identically, including in the browser via Codespaces — and the same practice pays off for day to day CAP, CPI tooling and UI5 development, where the environment is versioned with the code instead of living per-machine. 

The code is on GitHub: https://github.com/viksingh/sap-cpi-iflow-controller

entry.title: Configuring AWS Adapter Authentication: AssumeRole, AssumeRoleWithWebIdentity and IAMRolesAnywhere
entry.content:
When integrating AWS with SAP Cloud Integration, AWS Security Token Service (STS) enables the use of temporary security credentials associated with IAM roles. This approach reduces the risks associated with long-lived access keys and improves both the security and manageability of authentication.

In this blog, we will walk through the setup required to enable STS-based authentication for the AWS Adapter in SAP Integration Suite. For the AssumeRoleWithWebIdentity use case, we will use Microsoft Entra ID as an example OpenID Connect (OIDC) identity provider for the web identity federation scenario. We will also cover IAM Roles Anywhere, which enables workloads running outside AWS, such as SAP Integration Suite, to authenticate using X.509 certificates and obtain temporary AWS credentials without relying on long-lived access keys or third-party applications.

The AWS Adapter supports the following STS authentication methods:

  • STS (AssumeRole) – Allows a trusted AWS principal to assume an IAM role and obtain temporary security credentials.
  • STS (AssumeRoleWithWebIdentity) – Uses an OpenID Connect (OIDC) identity provider to exchange a web identity token for temporary AWS security credentials.
  • STS (IAM Roles Anywhere) – Uses X.509 certificates to enable workloads running outside AWS to securely assume IAM roles and obtain temporary AWS credentials.

This blog explains the configuration steps required for all approaches.

Let's start with STS (AssumeRole), which requires the least amount of configuration among the three authentication methods. In this approach, you create an IAM role that can be assumed by an IAM user. The IAM user's access key and secret key, together with the Role ARN, are then used to configure authentication in the AWS Adapter.

1. STS (AssumeRole) Setup

This setup directly links a role to an IAM user. No Azure configuration is required.

  1. Create IAM Role
    1. In AWS IAM, navigate to Roles and choose Create role.
    2. Select AWS account as the Trusted entity type.
    3. Specify the trusted AWS account ID that will be allowed to assume the role.
    4. Attach the required permission policies (for example, AmazonS3ReadOnlyAccess).
    5. Enter a role name and any optional description.
    6. Review the configuration and choose Create role.

  2. Update the Trust Relationship
    1. After creating the role, open it in the IAM console and navigate to the Trust relationships tab.
    2. Choose Edit trust policy.
    3. Update the policy to include the ARN of the IAM user (or principal) that is allowed to assume the role under the Principal element.
    4. Save the changes to update the trust relationship.

    Example trust policy JSON:

    {
    "Version": "2012-10-17", "Statement":[
    { "Effect": "Allow",
    "Principal":
    {
    "AWS": "arn:aws:iam::123456789012:user/demo-sts-user"
    },
    "Action": "sts:AssumeRole"
    }
    ]
    }

One of the most common causes of authentication failures is an incorrectly configured trust policy. This can happen if the IAM user is not added to the Principal element or if an External ID is required by the trust policy but is omitted from the AWS Adapter configuration.

As the final step, create a Secure Parameter security artifact in SAP Cloud Integration (CI) to securely store the Role ARN Alias. This alias can then be referenced in the AWS adapter configuration.

Next, we'll look at STS (AssumeRoleWithWebIdentity), which enables users authenticated by a third-party OpenID Connect (OIDC) identity provider to obtain temporary AWS credentials without requiring long-term AWS access keys.


2. STS (AssumeRoleWithWebIdentity) Setup

This setup uses Microsoft Entra ID (Azure AD) as an example identity provider.

Note: Supported identity providers using OpenID Connect (OIDC) can be used, such as Microsoft Entra ID, SAP IAS, or other compatible Web Identity Providers.

2.1 Microsoft Entra ID Setup

  1. Register Application
    1. Go to Microsoft Entra ID → App registrations → New registration.


    2. Provide a name, select the supported account types, and register the application.


       

    3. Note down the Tenant ID and Client ID from the overview tab.

       

  2. Add Client Secret
    1. Navigate to Certificates & secrets → New client secret.
    2. Create a secret and copy the Secret Value for later use.


  3. Set Application ID URI
    1. Go to Expose an API → Application ID URI.

       



    2. Set it to api://<client-id> (This will be set automatically).

  4. Configure Access Token Version
    1. In the application Manifest, set requestedAccessTokenVersion to 2.

2.2 AWS IAM Setup (Identity Provider & Role)

  1. Add OpenID Connect Identity Provider
    1. Log in to the AWS Management Console and navigate to IAM.
    2. In the left navigation pane, select Identity providers.
    3. Click Add provider.
    4. Configure the identity provider with the following details:
      • Provider Type: OpenID Connect

      • Provider URL:

        https://login.microsoftonline.com/<tenant-id>/v2.0

      • Audience:

        <client-id>
    5. Click Add provider to create the OpenID Connect (OIDC) identity provider.

  2. Create IAM Role for Web Identity
    1. Log in to the AWS Management Console and navigate to IAM.
    2. In the left navigation pane, select Roles.
    3. Click Create role.
    4. Under Trusted entity type, select Web identity.
    5. Configure the trust relationship:
      • Select the previously created OpenID Connect (OIDC) provider.
      • Select the appropriate Audience (<client-id>).
    6. Click Next.
    7. Attach the required IAM permission policies that grant access to the AWS resources your application or integration needs.
    8. Click Next.
    9. Enter a Role Name and, optionally, a description.
    10. Review the role configuration and click Create role.
    11. After the role is created, note the Role ARN, as it may be required for application configuration or role assumption requests.

2.3 SAP CPI Configuration

1. OAuth2 Client Credentials:

  1. In SAP Cloud Integration, navigate to Monitor → Security Material.
  2. Click Add and select OAuth2 Client Credentials.
  3. Maintain the following details:
     
    • Name: <Security Artifact Name>

    • Token Service URL:

      https://login.microsoftonline.com/<tenant-id>/oauth2/v2.0/token

    • Client ID:

      <client-id>

    • Grant Type:

      Send as Part of Body

    • Client Secret:

      <client-secret>

    • Scope:

      api://<client-id>/.default

    • Client Authentication:

      Send as Body Parameter

    • Content Type:

      application/x-www-form-urlencoded
  4. Save and deploy the security artifact.

  5. Reference this OAuth2 Client Credentials security artifact in the sender or receiver adapter by specifying it in the OIDC Credential Name field.

2. Secure Parameter (Role ARN)

  1. In SAP Cloud Integration, navigate to Monitor → Security Material.
  2. Click Add and select Secure Parameter.
  3. Store the Role ARN from AWS and deploy.
  4. Use the Role ARN Alias in the adapter configuration to enable AWS authentication.

Lastly, we'll look at STS (IAM Roles Anywhere), which enables workloads running outside AWS to assume IAM roles using X.509 certificates and obtain temporary AWS security credentials without relying on long-lived access keys.


3. STS (IAMRolesAnywhere) Setup

Note: In this guide, Keystore Explorer is used to create the certificates required for the IAM Roles Anywhere configuration.

3.1 Create a Key Pair for IAMRolesAnywhere Authentication

  1. Open KeyStore Explorer and create a new KeyStore; select PKCS #12 as the type.


  2. Click on Tools > Generate Key Pair. Retain default selections of RSA and a minimum Key Size of at least 2048 bits.


  3. Select Version 3 in the Generate Key Pair Certificate pop-up window.


  4. Click on   

     

    icon to edit name-related details.


  5. Click Add Extensions. You can add Certificate Extensions by clicking on "+".

     

3.2 Creating Key Pair: CERTIFICATE AUTHORITY

     6. For Certificate Authority, add the following extensions.

  1. Basic Constraints = critical, CA = TRUE

Note: The path length constraint in the Basic Constraints extension determines how many subordinate CA levels can exist below a CA certificate. Configure this value based on your intended certificate hierarchy.

    b. Key Usage(KU) = Certificate Signing, CRL Sign

   c. Subject Key Identifier (SKI) = NOT CRITICAL

Note: Click on the key icon to select a Generation Method.



  7. Enter the name as ROOT_CA.
  8. Enter and Confirm Password.
  9. You will get a message notifying you of Key Pair Creation.

 

3.3 Creating Key Pair: CLIENT

Note: Repeat Steps 1–5 from the section above, then continue from Step 10 as shown below.

10. For Client Certificate, add the following extensions.

a. Basic Constraints = critical, CA = FALSE



b. Key Usage (KU) = Digital Signature

c. Subject Key Identifier (SKI) = NOT CRITICAL

Note: Click on the key icon to select a Generation Method.

11. Enter the Alias as CLIENT.
12. Enter and Confirm Password.
13. You will get a message notifying Key Pair Creation.

3.4 Fetching the PEM from CERTIFICATE_AUTHORITY

  1. Navigate your ROOT_CA certificate in KeyStore Explorer.
  2. Double-click on the certificate > PEM > Copy.
    This will be required at a later step.

3.5 Configurations in AWS

Note: Before proceeding with this section, ensure you have completed the previous steps to create CLIENT and CERTIFICATE_AUTHORITY certificates and copied the PEM.

  1. Fetch the PEM that you copied in the CERTIFICATE AUTHORITY section and paste it in the External certificate bundle while creating the Trust Anchor. For more information, see Create Trust Anchor.
  2. Next, you must update the trust policy to include rolesanywhere.amazonaws.com and create a Profile under Role. For more information, see Configure Role and create Profile.

3.6 Generating the CSR using CLIENT

  1. Navigate to your CLIENT certificate in KeyStore Explorer.
  2. Right-click on the certificate > Generate CSR.
  3. Save it for signing CA later. This will be required at a later step.

3.7 Signing CLIENT using CERTIFICATE_AUTHORITY

  1. Navigate to your ROOT_CA certificate in KeyStore Explorer.
  2. Right-click on the certificate > Sign CSR.
  3. In the Choose CSR dialog, select the CSR created using CLIENT.
  4. In the Sign CSR dialog, click Transfer extensions and click OK.

Note: Transfer Extension allows you to skip the Add Extensions step by copying extensions from the Client.

    5. Next, you will see an Export Certificate Chain ‘client‘. Ensure Export Length is selected as Entire Chain and Export Format is X.509.

   6. You will be able to see a prompt that notifies you about successful CSR Signing.

Note: A Root CA can sign one or more Intermediate CAs, which can then issue client (leaf) certificates, forming a trusted certificate chain from the leaf certificate to the Root CA

3.8 Importing the CA Reply to CLIENT

  1. Navigate your CLIENT certificate in KeyStore Explorer.
  2. Right-click on the certificate >> Import CA Reply.
  3. Select your .cer file and click Import.


  4. You will get the following prompt if the previous step is successful.


  5.  Further, if you double-click on the Client certificate, you can see the hierarchy showing that CLIENT has been signed by ROOT_CA.

3.9 Exporting the Client Certificate

  1. Navigate your CLIENT certificate in KeyStore Explorer.
  2. Right-click on the certificate >> Export Key Pair.
  3. Select Format as PCKS#12 and specify a Password.

Note: You will be required to specify this password during Key Pair upload in SAP Integration Suite.

       4. A successful export will be confirmed by the following prompt.


       5. The final step is to upload the .pfx file to the SAP Keystore. For more information, see Uploading a Key Pair in SAP Keystore.

Note: All IAM Roles Anywhere resources are regional, and they must be created in the same account and region to be used together.

Conclusion

The authentication method you choose ultimately depends on your organization's security requirements and how workloads outside AWS are expected to access AWS resources. While all three approaches use AWS Security Token Service (STS) to issue temporary security credentials, they differ in how the requesting workload is authenticated.

If your organization already uses IAM users and is comfortable managing long-lived AWS access keys, STS (AssumeRole) is the simplest option. It allows you to combine an IAM user's access key and secret key with a restricted IAM role to obtain temporary credentials, making it a straightforward upgrade from static IAM user permissions.

If your organization wants to eliminate long-lived AWS access keys and already has an OpenID Connect (OIDC) identity provider, such as Microsoft Entra ID or SAP IAS, STS (AssumeRoleWithWebIdentity) is a better choice. It enables workloads to authenticate using identity tokens issued by the trusted identity provider, removing the need to store AWS credentials.

Finally, if your organization prefers not to depend on either long-lived AWS access keys or a third-party identity provider, STS (IAM Roles Anywhere) provides a certificate-based authentication model. Although it requires additional setup to establish a certificate trust chain, it is well-suited for workloads running outside AWS that require strong, certificate-based authentication without relying on external identity providers.

By understanding the strengths and trade-offs of each authentication method, you can choose the approach that best aligns with your organization's security policies, operational requirements, and infrastructure.

entry.title: Enabling IAS for SAP SuccessFactors RCM and RMK(Internal Career Site)
entry.content:
Introduction

In this blog post, I will walk through the process of enabling Identity Authentication Service (IAS) for SAP SuccessFactors Recruiting Management (RCM) and Recruiting Marketing (RMK) for an Internal Career Site. While the configuration steps are straightforward, ensuring that each component is correctly aligned is critical, as small inconsistencies can lead to issues such as login loops, failed redirects, or incomplete authentication.

This guide covers the required prerequisites, configuration steps, and troubleshooting scenarios based on practical observations to help you implement a secure and seamless authentication experience.

 

Prerequisites

Before enabling IAS for the Internal Career Site, ensure the following prerequisites are completed.

1. Identity Authentication Service (IAS)

IAS must be enabled for your SAP SuccessFactors instance. IAS acts as the Identity Provider and handles user authentication. Without IAS, SAML-based login for the Internal Career Site cannot be configured.

2. Career Site Builder Setup

Career Site Builder (CSB) must be configured and integrated with Recruiting Management (RCM). This integration enables seamless navigation and authentication between the career site and recruiting modules.

3. Recruiter Single Sign-On (SSO)

Recruiter SSO should be enabled to provide a consistent authentication experience across Recruiting Management and Recruiting Marketing.

If Recruiter SSO is not maintained, recruiters will not experience true single sign-on. Even after successfully logging in, they will be prompted to enter their username and password again when navigating between interconnected modules such as RCM and RMK. This breaks the seamless user experience and can create confusion, especially during testing, as it may appear that IAS is not functioning correctly.

4. Career Site and Recruiting Management Integration

Career Site–RCM redirects must be enabled.

Navigation:
Provisioning → Configure Recruiting Marketing Datacenter URL

Maintain the Recruiting Marketing datacenter URL that corresponds to your tenant region. 

If the datacenter URL is incorrect, users may experience failed redirects when accessing the internal career site. In practice, this can appear as the page not loading correctly or users being redirected to an invalid or blank page. In some cases, authentication seems to initiate but does not complete, which can be misleading since the issue is related to incorrect endpoint configuration rather than IAS itself.

5. Career Site Builder Integration Settings

Navigation:
Admin Center → Manage Career Site Builder → Site Configuration → Site Integration

Verify the following configurations in Career Site Builder Integration Settings:

  • Company ID
    Copy the Company ID from Provisioning. In training instances, ensure it is maintained in ALL CAPS.

  • Deployment Data Center
    Select the correct data center where your SAP SuccessFactors HCM instance is hosted.

  • Single Sign-On Security Key
    Maintain the security key (20–28 characters). This key acts as a bridge between RCM and RMK and is required for secure communication.

  • Recruiter Sync (Single Sign-On)
    Enable Recruiter Sync to allow synchronization of recruiter information and provide access to Career Site Builder and Recruiting Advanced Analytics.

In practice, when any of these configurations are incorrect or inconsistent, the integration between RCM and RMK does not fail immediately but leads to issues during usage. Recruiter Sync may not function properly, recruiters may be unable to access Career Site Builder, and job or candidate data may not synchronize as expected. These issues can be misleading, as the initial configuration appears complete, but the breakdown in connectivity between systems becomes visible only when recruiters start working with the system.

6. Mobile Apply and Internal Career Site

Ensure both Mobile Apply and Internal Career Site are enabled.

Navigation:
Admin Center → Manage Recruiting Sites

These settings allow employees to access and apply for internal jobs. If the Internal Career Site is not enabled, users will not be able to access internal opportunities even if IAS is configured successfully.


Step 1: Configure SAP SuccessFactors (RCM) for IAS

The first step is to establish trust between SAP SuccessFactors and Identity Authentication Service (IAS). This allows SuccessFactors to redirect users to IAS for authentication and accept the SAML assertion returned after a successful login.

Navigation

Go to:

Admin Center → Manage Service Provider Configuration for Identity Authentication Service

Enable SAML Integration

Under the Import IdP SAML Metadata section:

  1. Turn on SAML Integration
  2. Maintain the IAS Tenant Name

Note:
The tenant name is derived from the IAS URL.

Example:

https://a122u123.accounts.cloud.sap

Tenant Name: a122u123

An issue we encountered during this step was maintaining an incorrect tenant name. Although the configuration appeared to be correct, it resulted in an endless login loop where users were redirected to IAS, entered their credentials, and were sent back to the login page again instead of being authenticated. This made it difficult to identify the root cause initially, as the issue appeared to be related to authentication rather than a simple tenant mismatch.

Export Metadata

After completing the configuration:

  1. Click Save
  2. Click Export Metadata

Expected outcome: The metadata file is successfully generated and available for download. This file will be imported into IAS when creating the RCM application.

Step 2: Configure IAS Application for Recruiting Management (RCM)

Next, create an application in IAS to establish the trust relationship with your SAP SuccessFactors Recruiting Management instance. This application will use the metadata exported in the previous step to enable SAML-based authentication.

Navigation

Go to:

Applications & Resources → Applications

Create New Application

Click Create and maintain the following details as shown in the configuration screen:

  • Display Name: RCM_Tenant_name
  • Home URL: Leave blank (or maintain if required by your landscape)
  • Type: SAP SuccessFactors solution
  • Parent Application: None
  • Organization ID: global
  • Protocol Type: SAML 2.0

After maintaining the above details, click Create.

Configure SAML 2.0 Settings

  1. Open the newly created application
  2. Navigate to SAML 2.0 Configuration
  3. Upload the metadata file that was exported from SuccessFactors (RCM system)

Configure Certificate Settings

  1. Go to the Certificates section
  2. Maintain:
    • Signing Algorithm: SHA-256

SHA-256 is the recommended signing algorithm and helps ensure secure SAML communication between IAS and SuccessFactors.

Maintain Subject Name Identifier

This is one of the most important settings in the configuration, as it determines how IAS identifies users when sending the SAML assertion to SuccessFactors.

Maintain the following values:

  • Source: Identity Directory
  • Attribute: Login Name

The attribute specifies which user field from IAS will be passed to SuccessFactors as the user's identifier. In our case, we used Login Name as the attribute, but this can vary depending on your organization’s configuration. It is important to ensure that the selected attribute aligns with the user identifier maintained in SuccessFactors, otherwise users may authenticate in IAS but not be able to access the system.

Summary

At this stage, IAS has been enabled for SAP SuccessFactors Recruiting Management (RCM), and the required SAML configuration has been completed. The system is now prepared for the next phase of the Internal Career Site authentication setup.

Step 3: Configure Career Site Builder (RMK) for IAS

After enabling IAS for Recruiting Management (RCM), the next step is to establish trust between Recruiting Marketing (RMK) / Career Site Builder (CSB) and IAS. This allows employees accessing the Internal Career Site to be redirected to IAS for authentication instead of using the default login mechanism.

Navigation

Log in to your SuccessFactors instance and go to:

Admin Center → Manage Career Site Builder

Then navigate to:

Settings → IDP Configuration

Maintain IAS Tenant Details

In the Import IDP SAML Metadata section:

  1. Enter the IAS Tenant Name
  2. Click Save

The tenant name identifies which IAS tenant RMK should use for authentication. If the tenant name is incorrect, users may be redirected to the wrong authentication endpoint or encounter SAML-related errors when attempting to access the Internal Career Site.

Note:
Make sure you save the configuration before proceeding to metadata generation.

Generate and Export Metadata

After saving:

  1. Click Generate
  2. Click Export Metadata

Expected outcome: RMK metadata is generated successfully and can be imported into the RMK application in IAS.

The exported metadata file contains the RMK Service Provider information that IAS requires to establish trust. Think of this metadata as the configuration blueprint that allows IAS and RMK to recognize and trust each other during the authentication process.

Step 4: Create IAS Application for RMK

With the RMK metadata available, the next step is to create a dedicated application in IAS. Although Recruiting Management (RCM) and Recruiting Marketing (RMK) are closely connected, each application requires its own trust configuration in IAS.

Log in to the SAP Cloud Identity Services (IAS) Admin Console.

Navigation

Go to:

Applications & Resources → Applications

Create New Application

Click Create and maintain the following details (as shown in your configuration screen):

  • Display Name: RMK_Tenant_name
  • Home URL: Leave blank (or maintain if required)
  • Type: SAP SuccessFactors solution
  • Parent Application: None
  • Organization ID: global
  • Protocol Type: SAML 2.0

Click Create.

Configure SAML 2.0 Settings

  1. Open the newly created RMK application
  2. Navigate to SAML 2.0 Configuration
  3. Upload the metadata file exported from Career Site Builder

This step completes the trust configuration from the IAS side and allows IAS to recognize authentication requests originating from RMK.

Configure Signing Settings

  1. Go to Certificates
  2. Verify:
    • Signing Algorithm: SHA-256

This ensures that SAML assertions exchanged between IAS and RMK are signed using a secure and supported algorithm.

Maintain Subject Name Identifier

Ensure consistency with the RCM configuration:

  • Subject Name Identifier
    • Source: Identity Directory
    • Attribute: loginName

Important Notes

  • Always save changes before generating metadata in Career Site Builder
  • Separate applications must be created in IAS for:
    • RCM
    • RMK
  • Ensure the same Subject Name Identifier configuration is maintained across all applications
  • Ensure that the IAS application is created as a standalone application and not as a child application

Troubleshooting

Issue 1: Internal candidate unable to apply and repeatedly redirected to sign-in page

One issue I encountered during testing was an endless redirect loop when attempting to access the Internal Career Site. Authentication appeared to start correctly, but users were continuously returned to the sign-in page.

After investigation, the root cause was a tenant domain mismatch. The system was configured with the legacy domain format:

<tenant_name>.accounts.ondemand.com

However, the instance had already been migrated to the Common Super Domain (CSD) framework and was using:

<tenant_name>.accounts.cloud.sap

  • Ensure that this updated tenant name is maintained consistently in:

    • RCM (Manage Service Provider Configuration)
    • Career Site Builder → IDP Configuration

  • Save the changes and retry the access.

Issue 2: User successfully authenticates in IAS but cannot access the Internal Career Site

If users can log in through IAS but are not successfully signed in to SuccessFactors, verify the Subject Name Identifier configuration.

The attribute selected in IAS must correspond to the user identifier maintained in SuccessFactors. If the values do not match, IAS can authenticate the user successfully, but SuccessFactors will be unable to identify the corresponding account.

Review the Subject Name Identifier configuration in both the RCM and RMK IAS applications and confirm that the selected attribute aligns with your organization's user mapping strategy.

Conclusion

Enabling IAS for SAP SuccessFactors Recruiting Management (RCM) and Recruiting Marketing (RMK) requires configuration across multiple components, including SuccessFactors, Career Site Builder, and IAS. While the setup itself is straightforward, attention to details such as tenant configuration, metadata exchange, and user identifier mapping is critical for a successful implementation.

By following the steps outlined in this guide, you can establish the trust relationships required for SAML-based authentication and provide a seamless sign-on experience for internal career site users. If issues arise, validating tenant settings and authentication mappings early in the troubleshooting process can help resolve most common configuration problems.

entry.title: Using RFC Function Modules and BAPIs in SAP Build Process Automation
entry.content:
Hi Everyone!

Recently, I was working on a requirement to generate an SD Billing Document in SAP S/4HANA using SAP Build Process Automation.

Due to some internal requirements & technical restrictions, the only option available to me was to use the BAPI_BILLINGDOC_CREATEMULTIPLE BAPI to create the billing document.

To use this BAPI in SAP Build, there are two main configurations required:

1. Map the virtual host to the internal host in SAP Cloud Connector using the RFC protocol, and expose the required BAPI or RFC function module.

2. Create a destination of type RFC in the SAP BTP Cockpit.

Let's first see how Cloud to On-Premises mapping in Cloud Connector looks like:

We also need to enable the required resources, such as the BAPIs and function modules that we want to consume in SAP Build. The RFC_FUNCTION_SEARCH function module enables the search for function modules from SAP Build.



And this is how the destination in BTP looks:

The virtual message server is configured in the Cloud Connector and mapped to the internal SAP S/4HANA host, and logon group information is available in SMLG.



Logon group can be found here :

Now that the Cloud Connector and BTP destination configurations are complete, let’s log in to SAP Build and see how we can create an action using BAPI_BILLINGDOC_CREATEMULTIPLE.

Select RFC's, BAPI's:



All the BAPIs & RFCs from backend will be available to use. We will go ahead and look for BAPI/RFC we'd like to use, and create a new project.

The action is now available in Connectors -> Actions, and can be used in our build projects.



Using this action, I created a custom process that can be triggered to generate a billing document by entering the reference sales document number.



Let's see it in action;
Assume we have below sales order, for which we'd like to generate a billing document.



Let’s now access the custom process we created. I have added the form URL to SAP Build Work Zone for easy access.




Since I added an email notification step to the process, we receive an email once the billing document is generated successfully.



Let's validated the generated billing document number in the system.



That concludes the demo. Thanks for reading!

Stay tuned for more demos on AI agents using Joule Studio, n8n, and automation in SAP Build.

entry.title: Modernizing SAP Journal Entry Integration: From SOAP to REST with API Management
entry.content:
Introduction

Standard SAP Journal Entry APIs are typically exposed as SOAP services. While they work well within SAP landscapes, they often introduce challenges when integrating with external systems, especially those expecting REST-based interfaces.

In a recent project, this approach was used to expose Journal Entry functionality to an external non-SAP application, where the SOAP interface introduced integration complexity and increased development effort.

Understanding the Standard Journal Entry API

SAP provides standard SOAP-based APIs for Journal Entry processing (such as JournalEntryCreateRequestConfirmation_In), enabling the creation of accounting documents in S/4HANA.

For reference, SAP official API documentation can be found here: Journal Entry - Post (Synchronous)

Example of SOAP payload structure

Why This Becomes a Challenge

In many integration scenarios, especially when exposing services to external systems or partners, SOAP-based interfaces introduce a few challenges:

  • Complex XML payload structure
  • Larger message sizes
  • Harder consumption for non-SAP systems
  • Higher processing and transformation effort

These aspects can directly impact both development effort and operational costs, particularly in high-volume scenarios.

A REST-Oriented Integration Approach

To address these challenges, an integration layer was introduced using SAP Integration Suite (Cloud Integration), enabling transformation from SOAP to REST.

High-level architecture: REST exposure over standard SAP SOAP APIs using Integration Suite

Example Request Transformation

The REST payload was designed using a simplified and standardized naming convention aligned with common SAP terminology:

{
  "journalEntry": {
    "documentReferenceID": "REF_001",
    "companyCode": "1000",
    "documentDate": "2026-01-15",
    "postingDate": "2026-01-15",
    "items": [
      {
        "glAccount": "400000",
        "amount": 150.00,
        "itemText": "Expense Posting 01",
        "assignmentReference": "REF",
        "accountAssignment": {
          "internalOrder": "900123",
          "costCenter": "CC1000"
        }
      },
      {
        "glAccount": "200000",
        "amount": -150.00,
        "itemText": "Expense Posting 02",
        "assignmentReference": "REF",
        "accountAssignment": {
          "profitCenter": "PC1000"
        }
      }
    ]
  }
}

The payload structure was simplified using standardized and generalized field naming to focus on readability and integration usability, without exposing real business data.

Below is an example of how the simplified REST payload is transformed into the SOAP structure required by the standard SAP API, highlighting the mapping between a consumer-friendly model and the SAP-specific format:

<soap:Envelope xmlns:soap="http://schemas.xmlsoap.org/soap/envelope/">
  <soap:Body>
    <ns0:JournalEntryBulkCreateRequest xmlns:ns0="http://sap.com/xi/SAPSCORE/SFIN">      
      <MessageHeader>
        <CreationDateTime>2026-01-15T10:00:00.000Z</CreationDateTime>
      </MessageHeader>
      <JournalEntryCreateRequest>
        <MessageHeader>
          <CreationDateTime>2026-01-15T10:00:00.000Z</CreationDateTime>
        </MessageHeader>
        <JournalEntry>
          <OriginalReferenceDocumentType>BKPFF</OriginalReferenceDocumentType>
          <BusinessTransactionType>RFBU</BusinessTransactionType>
          <AccountingDocumentType>SA</AccountingDocumentType>
          <DocumentReferenceID>REF_001</DocumentReferenceID>
          <CreatedByUser>API_USER</CreatedByUser>
          <CompanyCode>1000</CompanyCode>
          <DocumentDate>2026-01-15</DocumentDate>
          <PostingDate>2026-01-15</PostingDate>
          <Item>
            <GLAccount>400000</GLAccount>
            <AmountInTransactionCurrency currencyCode="BRL">150.00</AmountInTransactionCurrency>
            <DocumentItemText>Expense Posting 01</DocumentItemText>
            <AssignmentReference>REF</AssignmentReference>
            <AccountAssignment>
              <OrderID>900123</OrderID>
              <CostCenter>CC1000</CostCenter>
            </AccountAssignment>
          </Item>
          <Item>
            <GLAccount>200000</GLAccount>
            <AmountInTransactionCurrency currencyCode="BRL">-150.00</AmountInTransactionCurrency>
            <DocumentItemText>Expense Posting 02</DocumentItemText>
            <AssignmentReference>REF</AssignmentReference>
            <AccountAssignment>
              <ProfitCenter>PC1000</ProfitCenter>
            </AccountAssignment>
          </Item>
        </JournalEntry>
      </JournalEntryCreateRequest>
    </ns0:JournalEntryBulkCreateRequest>
  </soap:Body>
</soap:Envelope>

The goal was to make the API easier to consume while preserving compatibility with the standard SAP interface.

Implementation in SAP Integration Suite

The transformation was implemented using standard Cloud Integration components such as Content Modifier and Message Mapping. In this specific scenario, Message Mapping was sufficient to handle the required transformations, without the need for additional Groovy scripting.

The integration flow was responsible for:

  • Receiving REST requests (JSON)
  • Transforming payloads into SOAP XML
  • Calling the standard SAP API
  • Handling responses and errors consistently

Special attention was required when handling nested structures such as account assignments, where JSON elements needed to be carefully mapped into the corresponding SOAP hierarchy.

Integration Flow

Error Handling

Error handling was a key aspect of the integration design, ensuring that technical SOAP faults were translated into clear and consistent REST responses for API consumers.

Different HTTP status codes (such as 400, 401, 403, and 500) were mapped based on the error context, providing more meaningful responses aligned with REST principles.

The following Groovy script was used within the integration flow to standardize error responses:

import com.sap.gateway.ip.core.customdev.util.Message;

def Message processData(Message message) {

    def status = message.getHeader("CamelHttpResponseCode", Integer)
    def exceptionMessage = message.getProperty("CamelExceptionCaught")?.getMessage()    

    if (!status) {
        if (exceptionMessage?.contains("Validation")) {
            status = 400
        } else {
            status = 500
        }
    }

    message.setHeader("CamelHttpResponseCode", status)
    message.setHeader("Content-Type", "application/json")

    def response

    switch (status.toString()) {
        case "400":
            response = """{"error":"BAD_REQUEST", "message":"Invalid payload","details":"${exceptionMessage ?: ""}"}"""
            break

        case "401":
            response = '{"error":"UNAUTHORIZED","message":"Authentication failure with backend system"}'
            break

        case "403":
            response = '{"error":"FORBIDDEN","message":"Access denied to backend system"}'
            break

        case "500":
            response = '{"error":"INTERNAL_ERROR","message":"Error processing request in backend system"}'
            break

        default:
            response = '{"error":"UNKNOWN_ERROR","message":"Unknown error"}'
    }

    message.setBody(response)

    return message;
}

In addition to technical error handling, business validation errors returned by the SAP backend were also mapped into structured JSON responses.

Example of business error response:

{
  "journalEntryResponse": {
    "documentInfo": {
      "accountingDocument": "0000000000",
      "companyCode": "1000",
      "fiscalYear": "2026"
    },
    "messages": [
      {
        "code": "RW609",
        "message": "Error in document: BKPFF (S4 system)"
      },
      {
        "code": "RW033",
        "message": "FI/CO interface: balance in transaction currency is not zero (BRL -0.01)"
      }
    ]
  }
}

This approach avoids exposing raw SOAP fault structures directly to consumers and provides a more consistent, readable, and predictable API behavior.

It also improves integration debugging by delivering structured and meaningful error messages that can be easily consumed by external systems.

Results and Benefits

This approach resulted in several improvements:

  • Easier consumption for external systems
  • Reduced payload size compared to standard SOAP structures
  • Better alignment with API-first and REST-first strategies
  • Improved maintainability and reusability

In addition, a simple comparison between SOAP and REST payloads highlighted a significant reduction in message size.

In this scenario, the SOAP payload was 2,454 bytes, while the equivalent REST payload was 740 bytes — representing a reduction of approximately 70% in message size.

SOAP vs REST payload size comparison

In high-volume scenarios, reducing message size can also contribute to lower processing and integration costs.

Design Considerations and Trade-offs

While simplifying the API, some trade-offs were considered. SOAP fault responses contain detailed technical information, which may not be fully exposed in REST responses.

This required balancing usability for external consumers with the need for sufficient diagnostic detail.

API Management Considerations

The API was exposed through SAP API Management to enable:

  • Security and authentication
  • Monitoring
  • Version control

API Management Overview

Final Thoughts

This was an interesting exercise in modernizing standard SAP APIs for broader consumption scenarios. The approach was successfully implemented in a real customer scenario, proving effective in improving integration usability.

I would be interested to hear how other teams are handling similar challenges.

👉Feel free to share your approach or exchange ideas on similar integration scenarios.

entry.title: CICD Actions for SAP Integration Suite / The Release Import workflow
entry.content:
The previous post covered how externalized parameters and log levels are stored in Git alongside each integration package, and how the pipeline applies the right values automatically on every deployment. At the end, it noted that the Download → Git → Deploy workflow intentionally stops at TST. This post is about what happens next: how a team takes a controlled, auditable snapshot of everything that has changed since the last release and promotes exactly that delta to production — no more, no less.

  • The Branch Model Revisited
  • Two Workflows, One Controlled Sequence
  • Step 1: Create Release — Cutting the Snapshot
    • What Happens Under the Hood
    • Simulation Mode
  • Step 2: Release Import — Deploying the Delta
    • What You Fill In
    • Analyze Changes: The Delta Calculation
    • Dry Run Before You Commit
    • Execute Deployment
  • What It Does Not Do
  • Key Takeaways

The Branch Model Revisited

The Git branch model was introduced in the Download → Git → Deploy post. It is worth briefly revisiting here, because the Release Import workflow is built directly on top of it.

The repository has two permanent branches.

The development branch is where all active work lands. Every time a developer runs the Download → Git → Deploy workflow, the changes go here. This branch is in constant motion during an active development cycle: packages are downloaded, parameters are updated, new iFlows appear, old ones are removed. It reflects the current state of the DEV tenant in Git.

The main branch represents what has been formally released. Between releases, it is untouched. It holds the state of the repository at the point of the last release — a clean, stable snapshot that every team member can trust as the baseline for production.

The gap between these two branches is the release. When the team is ready to promote, that gap needs to be calculated, reviewed, and deployed. That is exactly what the two release workflows do.

development  ─── [download A] ─── [update params] ─── [download B] ─── HEAD
                                                                           │
                                                                           │  Create Release:
                                                                           │  · merges development → main
                                                                           │  · creates Git tag v1.4.0
                                                                           │  · resets development to main
                                                                           ▼
main         ──────────────────── [v1.3.0] ──────────────────────── [v1.4.0]
                                      │                                    │
                                   base-ref                           target-ref
                                      └──────── Release Import ───────────┘
                                                compare delta, deploy to PRD

Two Workflows, One Controlled Sequence

The release process involves two separate workflows that are run in sequence.

Create Release (git-create-release.yml) is an admin workflow that cuts the snapshot. It merges the development branch into main, creates a Git tag and a GitHub Release, and resets development back to the state of main. The result is a clean, immutable reference point in Git — a tag that will never change and that precisely identifies everything in that release.

Release Import (btp-release-import.yml) is the deployment workflow. It takes two Git references — a base (the previous release tag) and a target (the new release tag) — computes what changed between them, and deploys only those changes to the specified environment. Nothing that did not change is touched.

The separation is deliberate. The snapshot and the deployment are two independent decisions. A team might create the release tag on a Friday and run the import to production on Monday after a final sign-off. The tag is immutable; the deployment can be triggered whenever the team is ready.

Step 1: Create Release — Cutting the Snapshot

The (Admin) GIT Create Release workflow is triggered manually. The inputs are minimal: a release name (which becomes the Git tag, for example v1.4.0) and an optional simulation flag.

  Two inputs: a release name (which becomes the Git tag) and an optional simulation toggle. Everything else is automated.

What Happens Under the Hood

The workflow executes a carefully sequenced set of steps to ensure the release is atomic and recoverable.

Step 1  Check duplicate tag
        └─ if v1.4.0 already exists → stop immediately

Step 2  Rename development → tmp/release-v1.4.0
        └─ takes development out of service during the release cut
        └─ provides recovery point if the run is interrupted
           (restart-safe: re-running the workflow picks up from here)

Step 3  Create pull request: tmp/release-v1.4.0 → main
        └─ programmatic merge preserves full commit history on main
        └─ every download and parameter change from the cycle is auditable

Step 4  Guard against duplicate merge on same commit
        └─ safety check before writing the tag

Step 5  Create Git tag v1.4.0 on main
        └─ publish GitHub Release at that tag
        └─ immutable reference — will never change

Step 6  Recreate development from main at v1.4.0
        └─ next cycle starts from a clean, known baseline

Step 7  Delete tmp/release-v1.4.0
        └─ staging branch cleaned up

A pull request is used for the merge — even though it is merged programmatically — because it preserves the individual commit history from development on main. Every package download, every parameter change, every commit that accumulated during the release cycle is visible in the history of main after the merge. This matters for auditability: the Git history of main tells the full story of how the release was assembled.

Restart safety. If the workflow is interrupted at any point, re-running it with the same release name resumes from where it stopped. The phase detection at the start of the workflow checks whether the release tag exists, whether the staging branch is still present, and whether development needs to be recreated — and picks up accordingly. No manual cleanup is needed if a run fails partway through.

Simulation Mode

Running the workflow with simulation set to true runs a dry-run that stops short of making any permanent change. The workflow creates a pull request from development into main to check mergeability, reports whether the merge would succeed or fail due to conflicts, and then closes the PR without merging. No branch is renamed. No tag is created. No release is published.

This is useful as a pre-check during an active release cycle — a way to verify that development is clean and mergeable before committing to the release.

Step 2: Release Import — Deploying the Delta

With the release tag in place, the team is ready to deploy. The (Admin) BTP Release Manager workflow calculates what changed and, optionally, deploys it.

What You Fill In

The workflow takes four inputs:

  • GIT Base Reference — the starting point of the comparison. For a standard production release this is the previous release tag, for example v1.3.0. For the very first release ever, it would be the initial commit or an early tag that represents the state of production before the pipeline took over.
  • GIT Target Reference — the ending point. This is the new release tag, for example v1.4.0, or the development branch for a TST promotion.
  • Perform deploy — false to calculate the delta and report it without deploying, true to deploy.
  • Target BTP Environment — the environment to deploy to, selected from the configured GitHub Environments.

Four inputs. The base and target refs define the comparison window; perform-deploy controls whether the workflow stops at analysis or proceeds to deployment.

Not only for PRD. The base-ref and target-ref inputs are Git references — not environment names. Nothing in the workflow limits their use to a production promotion. A team can use the Release Import workflow to promote from TST to a pre-production stage, or to redeploy a specific historical tag to any environment. The most common pattern is previous-tag → new-tag for PRD, but the comparison window is fully flexible.

Analyze Changes: The Delta Calculation

Before any deployment decision is made, the workflow runs the Analyze Changes job. This is always executed, regardless of whether perform-deploy is true or false.

The job uses git diff to compare the two references across both the IntegrationPackages and PartnerDirectory directories in the repository. The result is four CSV outputs — the precise deployment manifest for this release:

CSV Scope What it contains

Package uploadIntegrationPackagesPackages added or modified between base-ref and target-ref — will be deployed
Package deleteIntegrationPackagesPackages present in base-ref but absent in target-ref — will be removed from the target environment
PD uploadPartnerDirectoryPartner Directory entries added or modified — will be deployed
PD deletePartnerDirectoryPartner Directory entries present in base-ref but absent in target-ref — will be removed

All four lists are uploaded as a workflow artifact and printed in the run log, so the team can review exactly what will happen before committing to the deployment.

 The four CSV sections printed to the run log. In this example, three packages are queued for upload, one Partner Directory entry for upload, and nothing is being deleted.

This is the answer to the question every release manager asks: what exactly is going to change? The delta calculation answers it precisely, from the Git history, before a single API call is made to BTP.

Dry Run Before You Commit

The recommended pattern for a production release is to run the workflow twice: first with perform-deploy: false to inspect the delta, then with perform-deploy: true to execute it.

Run 1 — Inspect
  perform-deploy: false
  │
  ├─ Analyze Changes (always runs)
  │   └─ produces 4 CSV lists, uploads artifact, prints to log
  └─ Execute Deployment — SKIPPED
      → Review the output: correct packages? Unexpected deletes?

Run 2 — Execute  (same base-ref and target-ref)
  perform-deploy: true
  │
  ├─ Analyze Changes (runs again — always)
  │   └─ re-calculates delta from Git
  └─ Execute Deployment — RUNS
      → Packages deployed, deletions applied, parameters set

Running the analysis twice adds only seconds to the process. The cost is negligible; the confidence it provides is not.

Execute Deployment

When perform-deploy is true, a third job runs after the analysis: Execute Deployment. It receives the four CSV lists from the Analyze Changes job and processes them in order.

The deployment uses the same logic as the Download → Git → Deploy workflow: clean before upload, deploy package artifacts in the order defined by their Rank configuration, apply environment-specific parameter values and log levels after activation. The source for the upload is the target-ref — the new release tag — so the content deployed is precisely what was tagged, not whatever happens to be on a branch at the time of the run.

Deletions are also processed. If a package or Partner Directory entry was present in the base-ref but absent in the target-ref, it is deleted from the target environment. Deletions travel through the pipeline automatically — there is no manual step to remember.

The customer exit hook (cx-derive-iflow-exclusions) is evaluated at deployment time, not at analysis time. This means if a team has a customer exit that dynamically excludes certain iFlows from deployment based on runtime state, those exclusions are calculated fresh on each deploy job execution. Re-running a failed deployment gets a fresh exclusion evaluation, not a stale one from a previous run.

What It Does Not Do

The Release Import workflow does not apply externalized parameters or log levels for packages that were not in the delta. If a package was already deployed to production from the previous release and nothing in that package changed, it is not touched. The parameters and log levels already on the target environment remain as they are. Only the packages that actually changed are processed.

The workflow also does not create or manage the Git tag itself — that is the responsibility of the Create Release workflow. The Release Import workflow is a consumer of tags, not a producer. This keeps the two concerns cleanly separated: snapshot management is one action, deployment is another.

Key Takeaways

  • The release process is two workflows in sequence: Create Release cuts an immutable snapshot (merges development → main, creates a Git tag, resets development); Release Import computes and deploys the delta between two tags.
  • The snapshot and the deployment are independent decisions. The tag is created once and is immutable; the deployment can be triggered at any time after that, against any target environment.
  • The delta calculation is always explicit and inspectable. The four CSV lists (packages to upload, packages to delete, Partner Directory entries to upload, Partner Directory entries to delete) are available as a workflow artifact before any deployment takes place.
  • The recommended pattern is two runs: first with perform-deploy: false to review the delta, then with perform-deploy: true to execute. The second run re-calculates the delta before deploying.
  • Deletions are automatic. A package or PartnerID removed from the repository between the base-ref and the target-ref will be deleted from the target environment without a separate manual action.
  • The Create Release workflow is restart-safe. If interrupted, re-triggering with the same release name resumes from the correct phase without manual cleanup.
  • The base and target refs are not limited to tags. Any Git ref can be used — a branch name,  or a tag. The most common pattern for production is previous-release-tag → new-release-tag, but the comparison window is flexible.

The next post in this series covers Access Policies as code: how governance rules are version-controlled, promoted through the pipeline, and applied consistently across environments — following the same Git-first pattern as integration packages.

Repository and documentation: https://github.com/SAP/cicd-actions-for-sap-integration-suite

Series overview: The Challenge: Large Platform, Demanding Operations

entry.title: API-Centric Integration in SAP Integration Suite: A new paradigm for API & Integration practitioner
entry.content:
This blog is co-authored with @artigopalan (PM Director, SAP Integration Suite)

Introduction

Working with SAP Integration Suite, you know the depth it brings across its two most valued capabilities: Cloud Integration (CPI), the engine behind message mediation, transformation, and process orchestration, and API Management, where APIs are governed, secured, protected, and published. Together, they power and integrate some of the most complex enterprise landscapes in the world. And yet, as mature and capable as each is in its own right, there's a natural question that practitioners regularly ask: what if these two capabilities truly converged? What if the design experience, the runtime, and the observability layer spoke a single language, one that felt native to both the integration developer and the API architect? That question is what API-Centric Integration is designed to answer.

One Coin. Two Sides. One Experience.

Practitioners have long known that API management and integration are not independent concerns. Every API is an integration surface. Every integration exposes a contract that could benefit from API-level governance. The separation has been more a consequence of how solutions and their technical stacks evolved than a reflection of how the work actually flows.

API-Centric Integration is built on a simple but powerful premise: the design language, the runtime, and the observability plane should be unified, empowered by AI. Not loosely coupled. Not bridged after the fact. Unified by design.

What This Means in Practice

A common design language

Customers can now define, secure, and manage APIs using a single, consistent authoring experience that natively supports mediation and augmentation steps. The modeling itself occurs on a newly introduced artifact type simply called the API, and this, too, has been consciously kept distinct. It is not the same as an IFlow in Cloud Integration, and it is not the same as the API proxy or API artifact you may know from the classic API Management functionality. It is a first-principles rethink of what an API artifact should look like when integration capability is native to it, not layered on top.

If you are a Cloud Integration practitioner, the concepts inside it will feel familiar: routing, transformation, enrichment, logging. If you are an API architect, the governance constructs like policies, rate limiting, and security schemes are first-class citizens. The two disciplines that have long lived on separate stacks, with separate tools and separate mental models, now meet inside a single artifact.

Underneath this experience runs a single, SAP-managed cloud-native runtime called the Integration Cell. It exists to make the unified design experience above possible, with the deployment, scaling, and execution details handled for you.

 

Agent-readiness, with MCP Gateway

This is perhaps the most forward-looking aspect of API-Centric Integration. APIs built within this new model can be elevated to MCP Servers, the protocol standard for exposing tools and data to AI agents and large language models, without additional development effort. It is a deliberate architectural choice to ensure that the APIs you build today are natively consumable by the agentic applications and Joule-powered workflows of tomorrow. The API you publish for a human consumer and the tools you expose to an AI agent are, again, two sides of the same coin. 

Follow this post to learn more about the MCP Gateway.

 

A single observability pane

One of the most persistent operational challenges in running both stacks has been complexity: separate monitoring dashboards, separate log pipelines, and no single place to understand what is actually happening across your API and integration landscape.

API-Centric Integration changes that. It runs on a single execution engine with a unified pipeline for logs and traces. When something needs attention, you look in one place.

But observability in API-Centric Integration goes further than just consolidating logs. The same unified pane brings in analytics capabilities that were previously either absent or fragmented. Usage patterns, traffic trends, and performance metrics across your APIs and MCP servers are visible together, in a single dashboard, broken down by the dimensions that actually matter: by API, by consumer, by agent, by tool.

For teams that need to stay ahead of problems rather than just react to them, the analytics layer includes AI-based anomaly detection that learns the normal traffic behaviour of your APIs and flags deviations before they become incidents. Alongside that, traffic predictions give you a forward-looking view of expected API call volumes, so capacity planning and resource decisions are grounded in data rather than guesswork.

The result is an observability experience that is not just unified, but genuinely intelligent. One place to look, whether you are investigating a live issue, reviewing consumption trends, or planning for what comes next.

A simplified commercial model

For integrations built with this new design, there is no double-counting across the API and integration layers. You are running one thing. You pay for one thing.

A foundation for AI Gateway capabilities

Beyond MCP, a rich AI Gateway will be integrated into this design experience over time, bringing model routing, token governance, and LLM observability into the same unified surface. The integration layer is becoming the AI governance layer. API-Centric Integration is where that convergence begins.

What this is, and what it isn't

API-Centric Integration is relevant for greenfield projects: new APIs, new integration flows, new architectures built on a clean slate. Existing APIs managed through the current API Management capability and existing CPI iFlows continue to operate exactly as they are.

Think of it as the recommended starting point for net-new work, the architecture you would choose if you were starting today, knowing what you now know about where integration and AI are heading.

One additional note on scope: the Integration Cell is a new runtime introduced specifically for this paradigm. It sits alongside, not on top of, your existing Cloud Integration runtime and Edge Integration Cell. These are not competing runtimes. They are the right tool for the right job, depending on where you are in your journey.

The bigger picture

SAP Integration Suite has always been the connective tissue of enterprise landscapes. What is changing is the nature of what needs to be connected and what needs to consume those connections. Business processes increasingly involve AI agents that require governed, observable, low-latency access to enterprise data and functions. In that world, the distinction between an integration runtime and an API runtime becomes not just unnecessary, but actively counterproductive.

API-Centric Integration is our answer to that shift: a unified foundation that respects the depth of what practitioners have built, while opening the door to a design paradigm that is ready for the agent-first era.

Getting started

API-Centric Integration is available now as part of SAP Integration Suite. Full product documentation, including a guided walkthrough of the API Artifact, policy modelling, and Integration Cell setup, is available on the SAP Help Portal:

API-Centric Integration - SAP Help Portal 

Rollout status

The rollout is actively in progress across SAP's managed landscape:

  • Trial landscape: Already updated and available for exploration today.
  • Stage 1 data centers: Already updated and available for exploration today.
  • Stage 2 data centers: Currently receiving software updates and feature enablement.

For the full rollout schedule and data center details, refer to SAP Note 3418154 .

If you are an existing SAP Integration Suite customer, no action is required on your part to receive the update. When the rollout reaches your data center, the capability will be available in your tenant. Your existing integration flows and API proxies are unaffected.
