We will be sharing more on hands-on guidance, and product deep-dives in the coming weeks. Questions and feedback are welcome in the SAP Community comments below.

entry.title: Built an MCP Server for SAP Cloud Integration
entry.content:
Introduction

I'm Jayesh Sinha, an SAP Integration Suite Architect with over 11 years of experience designing and implementing integration solutions across SAP and non-SAP landscapes. Throughout my career, I have worked extensively with SAP Cloud Integration (CPI), helping organizations build scalable, reliable, and maintainable integration flows. This hands-on experience with CPI's monitoring, troubleshooting, and operational challenges is what inspired me to explore how AI agents and the Model Context Protocol can simplify day-to-day integration work. This blog reflects my journey of combining enterprise integration expertise with modern AI tooling to make CPI operations more conversational and efficient.

SAP Cloud Integration, also known as SAP CI, is widely used for designing, deploying, and monitoring integration flows between SAP and non-SAP systems. While CPI provides strong monitoring and troubleshooting capabilities, checking message logs, failed messages, runtime artifacts, and integration packages usually requires manual navigation inside the SAP Integration Suite UI.

In this blog, we will build a Python-based MCP server that connects to SAP CI and exposes CI operations as tools. Then, we will connect an AI agent to this MCP server so the agent can inspect CI logs, identify failed messages, summarize issues, and help with troubleshooting.

Instead of manually opening CPI, filtering logs, and reading technical payloads, the AI agent can call predefined CPI tools through the MCP server and return a clear summary.

What Is MCP?

MCP stands for Model Context Protocol. It is a standard way for AI applications and agents to connect with external tools, systems, APIs, files, and data sources.

In simple terms, MCP allows an AI agent to use tools safely and consistently.

For example, instead of giving an AI model direct access to SAP CPI credentials and asking it to figure out API calls, we create an MCP server that exposes controlled tools

High-Level Architecture

User Queation -> AI Agent-> MCP Client -> Python MCP Server -> SAP CPI OData APIs -> CPI Logs, Artifacts, Packages, Metadata

The MCP server is responsible for:

Authenticating with SAP CPI
Calling CPI OData APIs
Handling JSON responses
Exposing CPI actions as MCP tools
Keeping credentials outside the AI prompt
Providing a controlled access layer

Benefits of This Approach

This solution provides several benefits.

It makes CPI troubleshooting more conversational. Developers can ask questions instead of manually navigating logs.

It separates AI reasoning from enterprise system access. The AI agent does not directly handle CPI credentials.

It creates a reusable integration layer. The same MCP server can be used by different MCP-compatible clients.

It improves maintainability. CPI API logic is centralized in one server.

It is extensible. More CPI tools can be added later as needed.

It supports safer automation. Read-only tools can be introduced first, while write operations can be added later with stronger controls.

Configuration

you can use CHATGPT/CLAUDE Desktop to connect to the MCP server.

1) Download MCP server in you local system from below GIT repository.

https://github.com/jacksnh/CPI-MCP-SERVER 

2) Modify "claude_desktop_config.json" to add the MCP server

"ci-mcp-server2": {
    "command": "python",
    "args": [
        "C:\\Users\\Username\\path\\to\\your\\server.py"
    ]
}

3) Restart Claude desktop and you will see below Server in Developer Section

4) Now your AI agent is ready to interact with MCP server

 

Conclusion

Integrating SAP Cloud Integration with an AI agent through an MCP server represents a meaningful step forward in how developers and integration teams interact with enterprise middleware. Rather than manually sifting through logs and navigating the SAP Integration Suite UI, teams can now ask plain-language questions and receive clear, actionable answers in seconds.

entry.title: MCP Gateway in SAP Integration Suite: Your APIs, Ready for the Age of Agents
entry.content:
This blog is co-authored with @artigopalan  (PM Director, SAP Integration Suite)

This post is a follow-up to our earlier introduction of API-Centric Integration. If you haven't read that yet, we recommend starting there.

Introduction

The way enterprise software gets used is changing. AI agents, autonomous programs that reason, plan, and take action, are no longer a future concept. They are arriving in production environments now, and they need to access your enterprise systems, data, and processes to be useful.

The question for every enterprise architect and IT leader is the same: how do you let AI agents into your systems without losing the governance and control you have spent years building?

In our previous post, we introduced API-Centric Integration: a unified design and runtime experience that brings API governance and integration mediation together in a single artifact. We closed with a forward-looking note on agent-readiness. This post is where that story continues.

MCP Gateway in SAP Integration Suite is the answer.

A quick glossary before we begin

MCP is still a relatively new term in most enterprise conversations, so it is worth grounding the three concepts that matter here before going further.

An MCP Tool is a named, callable entity that an AI agent can discover and invoke. Think of it as a function with a description: the agent reads that description, decides whether the tool is relevant to the task at hand, and calls it. A tool might represent "Get Customer Order Status," "Reconcile Inventory Levels Across Distribution Centers," or "Trigger a Purchase Order Approval." Tools are the atomic unit of agent-accessible functionality.

An MCP Server is the runtime host that exposes one or more tools over the Model Context Protocol, an open standard that any MCP-compatible agent or framework can connect to. If tools are the endpoints, the MCP Server is the API server hosting them. It makes tools discoverable and invokable in a standardized way, decoupled from how the underlying system actually works.

An MCP Gateway is the enterprise control plane for MCP. Where an MCP Server hosts tools, the Gateway governs access to them: enforcing authentication, authorization, rate limiting, and payload protection, and providing full monitoring and traceability. SAP Integration Suite's MCP Gateway aggregates tools from SAP APIs, non-SAP APIs, integration flows, data sources, and external MCP Servers into a single governed entry point.

The problem with the way things work today - and why your existing investment is the solution

Most enterprises that have invested in SAP Integration Suite already have a curated, governed library of APIs: connecting SAP S/4HANA, Business AI Platform, third-party systems, and custom backends. That represents years of work: designing integrations, securing them, documenting them, and operationalizing them.

The challenge until now has been that none of that investment was directly accessible to AI agents. Agents do not call REST APIs natively the way developers do. They need tools: described, discoverable, and callable via a standardized protocol. Without that, every agent project forces the same painful workaround: a developer manually reads the backend schema and translates it into the agent's tool-calling code, field names, types, nested structures, all hardcoded. That mapping is brittle. If the backend schema changes, nothing tells the agent. The integration breaks, silently. The next project repeats the same work from zero. And security and governance are only as consistent as each developer's diligence.

The agent can reason. It still cannot reach your systems without someone hand-building the bridge, every time.

MCP Gateway closes that gap without asking you to rebuild anything. Your curated APIs become agent-ready tools. Your existing governance models: RBAC, rate limits, and monitoring carry forward into the agentic layer. The work you have done is not a legacy to migrate away from. It is the platform your AI strategy runs on.

Introducing MCP Gateway

MCP Gateway in SAP Integration Suite is organized around three things:

Connect. Turn APIs and services into discoverable, reusable tools for agent use. API specifications are automatically translated into MCP schema and augmented with natural language semantics, so agents understand not just the structure of a tool but what it is for. No manual schema translation. No hardcoding. Backend connectivity follows familiar conventions: API Providers and BTP Destinations are used to discover and connect to SAP and non-SAP backend systems, so the connectivity model your teams already rely on carries forward into the agentic layer. Developer Hub extends naturally from an API catalog to a tool and MCP catalog: developers and agent builders can discover available MCP tools, understand their purpose and schema, and subscribe, using the same experience they already use for APIs

Govern. Apply policies, authentication, and usage controls for agents to access tools and systems. Rate limiting, IP blocking, authentication based on OIDC from SAP  (XSUAA) and third-party identity providers, and payload protection all apply to agent traffic with the same rigor as human-facing API traffic. Agents only see and can invoke the tools they are explicitly authorised for: essential in multi-agent environments where different agents have different roles and should have different access boundaries.

Analyze. Provide visibility and traceability across agents, tools, and enterprise landscapes. Every tool invocation by every agent is logged with full traceability, supporting compliance requirements, operational governance, and usage analytics. You have the same visibility into agent behavior that you have today into the transactions handled by SAP Integration Suite, including comprehensive logs, traces, and intelligent analytics.

 

MCP Gateway as the endorsed pathway for SAP API access

This is not a standalone capability sitting outside SAP's broader architecture thinking. SAP's Architecture Center reference architecture for A2A and MCP for Interoperability defines how enterprise AI agent ecosystems should be structured, and SAP Integration Suite's MCP Gateway sits at its core.

In practical terms, if you are deploying AI agents that interact with SAP systems, MCP Gateway is not just a convenient option. It is SAP's architecturally and policy-endorsed approach to doing so in a compliant, governed, and auditable way.

The recommendation is clear: external third-party agents should use MCP Gateway to access SAP systems. Customer-managed pro-code and low-code agents deployed in the SAP BTP context should use MCP Gateway to access SAP systems. Third-party MCP Servers should federate with the MCP Gateway to access SAP systems.

An important distinction: MCP is a connectivity protocol, not a semantic layer

The Architecture Center makes a point here that is easy to overlook and consequential if you do.

MCP standardizes how agents call tools and APIs. It does not add business context or improve the accuracy of AI results. Connecting an MCP server directly to raw SAP transactional APIs without semantic enrichment leads to poor entity discovery accuracy, excessive token consumption, and, for write operations, a real risk of incorrect business transactions. An agent that calls a raw OData endpoint without understanding what the fields mean, or without the business context to distinguish a purchase order from a blanket agreement, will reason poorly and act incorrectly.

This is why the design of an MCP tool matters as much as the platform that hosts it. MCP Gateway in SAP Integration Suite handles the governed, enterprise-grade concerns: authentication, authorization, rate limiting, payload protection, and observability across SAP and non-SAP APIs. What it does not do, and is not designed to do -> is to decide how to shape your tools.

An API Artifact that exposes nine atomic CRUD endpoints as nine separate tools will produce an MCP Server that forces an agent to invent the orchestration logic a good tool designer should have encoded. The right instinct is to design tools around business outcomes rather than API operations: find_at_risk_suppliers_for_material rather than GET /assmnt/PlantMaterialRisk/{plantId}. The platform enables either. The design is yours.

Two typical use cases

The following scenarios show what becomes possible when your existing SAP investment meets the agentic layer.

Use Case 1: Third-Party AI Platform Accessing SAP Business Data

A retailer uses Microsoft Copilot Studio to build procurement agents for their buying team. The agents need to query SAP product catalogs, check supplier agreements, and initiate purchase requisitions. Historically, this required a custom integration project where developers hand-translated backend schemas into tool-calling code.

With the MCP Gateway, SAP's procurement APIs are exposed as MCP tools through a hosted MCP Server. Copilot Studio agents connect via the standard MCP protocol, authenticate via OIDC, and invoke only the tools they are authorized for. The IT team governs access from a single control plane in Integration Suite, fully aligned with the endorsed architecture from SAP's Architecture Center.

Use Case 2: Multi-Agent HR Process Automation on SAP BTP

An enterprise deploys multiple specialized agents on SAP BTP for HR: one for onboarding, one for payroll queries, one for performance data. Each is built differently: the onboarding agent is low-code via Joule Studio (fka SAP Build), the payroll agent is pro-code using SAP Cloud SDK for AI.

Both agents access SAP SuccessFactors and third-party HR data sources via the MCP Gateway. Tool authorization is scoped per agent role: the onboarding agent cannot access payroll tools, and vice versa. The architecture follows SAP's golden path for agentic AI interoperability.

What it shields you from

MCP is still evolving rapidly. The specification has already undergone significant architectural changes in 2026. Running self-managed MCP servers means tracking that specification roadmap closely, managing upgrade cycles, and absorbing the security implications of each revision. Both patterns of self-managed MCP servers, whether hosted externally or on SAP BTP, place the full operational responsibility for platform stability, security hardening, credential lifecycle, and spec compatibility squarely on the customer or partner deploying them.

For a detailed breakdown of the cross-cutting concerns every MCP operator must address, including authentication, token exchange, rate limiting, observability, and lifecycle management, the Third-Party MCP Access reference architecture on SAP's Architecture Center is the definitive starting point.

The bigger picture

MCP Gateway in SAP Integration Suite is the natural evolution of the API Gateway into the agentic era. The core promise is the same: bring your systems together under governed, observable, secure access. The difference is that the consumer is no longer just a developer or an application. It is an AI agent.

SAP Integration Suite has always been the connective tissue of enterprise landscapes. MCP Gateway is what makes that connective tissue agent-aware. The APIs you have already built, the integration flows already running, the S/4HANA function modules already deployed: all of it can now be made consumable by agents through a single governed surface, with the same authentication, observability, and policy enforcement you apply to human-facing traffic today.

Getting started

MCP Gateway is available now as part of SAP Integration Suite Premium and Enhanced Editions. Full documentation is available on the SAP Help Portal. You can also get started by trying it out using the SAP BTP trial or the Free Service Plan.

We will be sharing deep dives and hands-on tutorials in the coming weeks. Questions and feedback are welcome in the SAP Community comments below.

entry.title: SAP BTP - CI - CITS Extension - Generate Technical Specifications Directly from the iFlow Designer
entry.content:
Update — CITS v1.1.1 Final Release - 18/07/2026

CITS has reached its final version.

The original objective remains unchanged: reduce repetitive documentation effort and generate a consistent technical specification baseline directly from a saved SAP Cloud Integration iFlow. However, the extension has evolved significantly since the initial publication of this article.

What changed in v1.1.1

  • The Generate Specification action is now integrated directly into the SAP Cloud Integration iFlow editor.
  • The action remains available in both Edit and Save modes.
  • CITS works independently and does not require SAP CPI Helper.
  • The configuration interface was redesigned with clearer sections and guided options.
  • Optional dynamic headers and footers can include the iFlow name, environment, document version, confidentiality classification, generation date, and Word page numbering.
  • Users can choose between DOC and DOCX output.
  • A document preview is available before export.
  • Document Control was moved to the cover page.
  • iFlow details and the direct SAP Integration Suite link are presented in a dedicated section.
  • Script, XSLT, Message Mapping, schema, and resource information was consolidated into a clearer resource inventory with Used and Not Referenced status.
  • Interface and adapter details include the technical information exposed by the saved design-time model, including ProcessDirect addresses when available.
  • The generated document was simplified to remove sections that did not provide enough reliable value.

CITS continues to process the iFlow information locally in the browser and does not use an external backend.

Version 1.1.1 is the final public release. No further feature updates or new versions are planned.

The remainder of this article documents the original motivation, architecture, capabilities, and intended usage of CITS. Some screenshots and references to the original floating button and DOC-only export represent earlier versions of the extension. The current release uses the integrated Generate Specification action and supports both DOC and DOCX.

Documentation should not take longer than the integration development itself. 

Introducing CITS: A Free Technical Specification Generator for SAP Cloud Integration

Anyone who has worked with SAP Cloud Integration for a while knows the documentation gap.

You spend time building a complex iFlow, validating router conditions, Content Modifiers, scripts, mappings, adapters, credentials, and resources. But then comes the repetitive part: documenting everything.

In real-world projects, this usually means taking screenshots, opening multiple steps manually, listing headers and properties, checking mapping logic, copying adapter details, and trying to keep a Word document aligned with the latest iFlow version.

This process is time-consuming, error-prone, and often becomes outdated as soon as the iFlow changes.

To help with this, I created CITS — Cloud Integration Technical Specification Generator.

I initially built it for my own delivery work: to improve the quality, consistency, professionalism, and technical depth of my documentation, while making it easier to extend or reuse local project templates.

Now I am sharing it with the SAP Community as a 100% free productivity browser extension.

CITS generates technical specifications directly from SAP Cloud Integration iFlows.

What CITS Does

CITS goes beyond simple screenshots. It analyzes the saved design-time model and generates a structured technical documentation baseline.

The generated specification can include:

  • Architecture overview diagram
  • Process summary
  • Sender and receiver/interface details
  • Adapter and security configuration summary
  • Content Modifier details
  • Headers and properties inventory
  • Router and routing condition summary
  • Script and mapping resources
  • Message Mapping field inventory
  • Resource usage inventory
  • Review notes and assumptions

The generated file is a Word-compatible .DOC document, so it can be reviewed, copied, adjusted, and shared according to each project’s documentation process.

Core Capabilities

Architecture Overview

CITS generates a high-level architecture diagram from the iFlow model, helping reviewers quickly understand the trigger or sender, main process, local processes, outbound calls, receiver systems, adapters, and protocols.

Process and Mapping Inventory

CITS extracts relevant technical details from Content Modifiers and Message Mappings, including headers, properties, source fields, target fields, direct mappings, standard functions, and UDF/custom functions.

Resource Usage Check

Large projects often contain old Groovy scripts, XSLT files, schemas, or mappings that nobody wants to delete because nobody is sure whether they are still used.

CITS includes a resource inventory to help identify resources that are used, referenced, or potentially unused.

Adapter and Security Summary

CITS summarizes technical references such as endpoints, queues, topics, credential aliases, certificate aliases, authentication methods, and connectivity details when they are available in the design-time model.

CITS floating button inside the SAP Cloud Integration iFlow designer.

How to Install and Use CITS

Installation is straightforward.

  1. Open Google Chrome.
  2. Go to the Chrome Web Store.
  3. Search for CITS.
  4. Click Add to Chrome and confirm the installation.
  5. Open your SAP Integration Suite tenant.
  6. Go to an integration package and open an iFlow.

Direct link: SAP CITS

When CITS detects an iFlow designer page, the floating action button appears inside the designer.

🔒Security & Transparency Note: Because CITS was just launched and my developer profile is new, Google Chrome's "Enhanced Safe Browsing" might display a temporary “Proceed with caution / Not considered trusted” popup when you click install.

As an Independent Architecture Researcher, I designed CITS with a 100% local processing architecture. The extension does not use any backend servers, does not expose any credential and transmits absolutely zero data outside your browser window. You can safely click "Continue installation". 

Generating Your First Document in 2 clicks

  1. Open the iFlow.
  2. Save the iFlow if you recently changed it.
  3. Click the CITS button.
  4. Refresh the captured model.
  5. Preview the generated technical specification.
  6. Download the Word-compatible .DOC file.
  7. Adjust the document if your company has an official template.

CITS does not require the iFlow to be deployed. It works from the saved design-time model.

Optional project metadata can be added before generating the technical specification.

iFlow Architecture in the Document

The architecture overview is designed to provide a clean technical view for documentation and peer review.

It is not intended to replace the SAP Cloud Integration graphical editor. Its purpose is to make the generated document easier to understand and review.

Real Iflow:

Canvas of the diagram in the document

 

Generated .DOC technical specification with an embedded iFlow architecture canvas.

Message Mapping Documentation

For Message Mapping scenarios, CITS can document the mapping structure in a searchable format.

The generated specification can include:

  • Source resource
  • Target resource
  • Source fields
  • Target fields
  • Direct mappings
  • Standard functions
  • UDF/custom functions
  • Mapping inventory table

This helps reduce the need to open the Message Mapping editor just to understand basic field-to-field transformations.

Message Mapping documentation with source fields, transformations, target fields, and UDF/standard function visibility.

Template Approach

CITS currently generates one standard technical specification template.

This is intentional.

Every company has its own documentation governance, approval sections, naming conventions, branding, and compliance wording. CITS is designed to generate a strong technical baseline, not to replace internal governance.

Recommended usage:

  1. Generate the CITS technical specification.
  2. Review the technical content.
  3. Copy relevant sections into the official company template if needed.
  4. Add project-specific approvals, governance notes, or business context.

Privacy and Security

CITS runs in the browser.

There is no external backend used to generate the document. The generated .DOC file is created locally from the iFlow information available in the browser session.

As always, companies should review browser extension usage according to their own internal security and compliance policies.

Limitations

CITS is a productivity tool, not an official SAP product.

A few important points:

  • Unsaved iFlow changes may not be reflected.
  • The iFlow should be saved before generating the document.
  • The generated specification should still be reviewed by an integration developer or architect.
  • Some adapter details depend on what SAP Cloud Integration exposes in the design-time model.
  • Company-specific governance sections may need to be added manually.

Final Thoughts

CITS was created to reduce repetitive technical documentation work for SAP Cloud Integration projects.

It does not replace solution design, functional specification, testing evidence, governance, or peer review. It helps generate a consistent technical baseline faster.

The objective is simple:

Less manual documentation effort. More consistent technical review.

I hope this helps SAP Integration developers, consultants, architects, and project teams dealing with recurring documentation requirements.

Feedback is welcome.

Related Independent Solo Research Deliverables and SAP SCN Community Contributions

For readers interested in the broader research path behind CITS, these are some of the topics I have been publishing about since January 2026:

To be completely honest, I am well aware that dense topics focused on deep architecture and real governance typically attract a low level of immediate digital engagement. The reason is simple: the content I publish requires a foundational level of structural abstraction and engineering mindset that goes far beyond the operational, "click-and-configure" routine most of the market is used to consuming.

However, vanity metrics do not dictate the intrinsic value of what is engineered. If any of the topics listed below has helped you view the integration ecosystem through a more mature lens—even if invisibly, without a single "like" or public reaction—then my objective has been completely achieved.

The year 2026 has been a period of intensive, solo, and entirely independent technical production. My sole motivation remains to deliver both intellectual frameworks and practical tools to elevate the technical baseline of integration developers within the SAP SCN Community, helping anyone who has the drive to break out of the comfort zone.

Beyond SAP CPI "Expert label" - Motivational journey

  • SAP BTP - IS - How to become a SAP Integration Suite Consultant beyond SAP CPI - SAP APIM Part I
  • SAP BTP - IS - How to become a SAP Integration Suite Consultant beyond SAP CPI - SAP EM/AEM Part II
  • SAP BTP - IS - How to become a SAP Integration Suite Consultant beyond SAP CPI - SAP IA - Part III
  • SAP BTP - IS - How to become a SAP Integration Suite Consultant - Part IV

Architecture and Governance

  • SAP CPI - Smart JMS Retry with Exception-Based Routing in SAP Integration Suite
  • SAP BTP - CI - Immutable Event Routing per Domain with Metadata-Driven Consumer Orchestration
  • SAP BTP - Integration Suite: Clean Architecture vs The Frankenstein Landscape

EventSmartKafka - Custom Kafka Adapter full open source - License Apache 2.0

Step by step how to create a custom adapter using SAP ADK for SAP Cloud Integration, in this case the adapter is for KAFKA integration

  • SAP BTP - CI - How I Built an Open-Source Enterprise Custom Kafka Adapter from Scratch - Part I
  • SAP BTP - CI - Building Open-Source Custom Adapter EventSmartKafka from Zero with SAP ADK - Part II
  • SAP BTP - CI - EventSmartKafka - Custom Kafka Adapter From .ESA - Validated Runtime - Part III
  • SAP BTP - CI - EventSmartKafka - Custom Kafka Adapter From .ESA v1.1.0 - Validated Runtime - Part IV

Github: EventSmartKafka - Custom Resources full open code and .esa for download 

SDIA - Applied in SAP - API Management ( GDCR + DDCR ) and SAP Cloud Integration ( ODCP )

  • SAP BTP - API Management with SDIA — GDCR & DDCR in Practice
  • SAP BTP - APIM - DDCR - Deterministic Routing Mechanism JavaScript Policy full explanation each step
  • SAP BTP - CI - Immutable Routing Principle: in SAP CPI with Value Mapping metadata ODCP in practice
  • SAP BTP - CI - Immutable Kafka Topic Routing Mechanism Using SDIA with ODCP for SAP CPI

Architectural decision DEIP and Governance Architecture SDIA - full open source - License CC BY 4.0 Internacional

Github: All tests of GDCR + DDCR using SDIA 

Site: www.domain-intent.com

DEIP — Domain Enterprise Integration Pattern - https://zenodo.org/records/19825678
└── Decision Pipeline
        → Intent
        → Interaction Type
        → Quality Profile
        → Integration Capability
